clojure.lang.ExceptionInfo: request timed out {:com.blockether.anomaly.core/category :com.blockether.anomaly.core/fault, :com.blockether.anomaly.core/message "request timed out", :type :svar.core/http-error, :llm-request {:model "gpt-4o", :base-url "https://llm.blockether.com/v1/chat/completions", :timeout-ms 180000, :api-key-length 35, :api-key-prefix "sk-bc99e", :messages [{:role "system", :content "<objective>\n<chain_of_density_iteration>\n    <task>Create a denser version of the previous summary by incorporating missing entities.</task>\n    \n    <process>\n        <step number=\"1\">Review the already-extracted entities list — do NOT re-extract any of them</step>\n        <step number=\"2\">Identify salient entities from the SOURCE TEXT that are missing from BOTH the entity list AND the previous summary</step>\n        <step number=\"3\">Rewrite the summary to incorporate these genuinely new entities while maintaining the same word count</step>\n    </process>\n    \n    <entity_criteria>\n    <criterion name=\"atomic\">Each entity must be a single named thing — a person, place, date, organization, concept, or term. NOT a phrase, clause, or description.</criterion>\n    <criterion name=\"relevant\">Related to the main story or central theme</criterion>\n    <criterion name=\"specific\">A proper noun, date, or precise term from the text — not a generic description or paraphrase</criterion>\n    <criterion name=\"faithful\">Must appear verbatim or near-verbatim in the source text — no invented modifiers or rewordings</criterion>\n    <criterion name=\"novel\">Not present in the previous summary or the already-extracted entities list</criterion>\n</entity_criteria>\n    \n    <guidelines>\n        <instruction>Make every word count by rewriting for better flow</instruction>\n        <instruction>Create space through fusion, compression, and removing fillers</instruction>\n        <instruction>Use ONLY information present in the source text — remove any interpretation, framing, or meta-commentary</instruction>\n        <instruction>Do NOT describe what the text is — summarize WHAT IT SAYS</instruction>\n        <instruction>The summary must be self-contained and understandable without the source</instruction>\n        <instruction>Missing entities can appear anywhere in the new summary</instruction>\n        <instruction>Never drop entities from the previous summary</instruction>\n        <instruction>If no space can be made, add fewer new entities rather than dropping old ones</instruction>\n        <constraint>Maintain approximately ~80 words</constraint>\n    </guidelines>\n    \n    <output_requirements>\n        <field name=\"entities\">Array of NEW entity objects added in this iteration, each with 'entity' (atomic name), 'rationale' (why it's salient), and 'score' (0.0-1.0 salience)</field>\n        <field name=\"summary\">The rewritten denser summary (~80 words)</field>\n    </output_requirements>\n</chain_of_density_iteration>\n</objective>"} {:role "user", :content "<source_text>\nVoyager 1, launched by NASA on September 5, 1977, from Cape Canaveral aboard a Titan IIIE-Centaur rocket, is the most distant human-made object from Earth. As of 2024, it is over 24 billion kilometers from Earth, traveling through interstellar space at approximately 17 kilometers per second. The spacecraft carries a Golden Record, a 12-inch gold-plated copper phonograph record containing sounds and images selected to portray the diversity of life and culture on Earth — curated by a committee chaired by Carl Sagan of Cornell University. Voyager 1 made its closest approach to Jupiter on March 5, 1979, discovering volcanic activity on Io and revealing the intricate structure of Jupiter's ring system. It then flew past Saturn on November 12, 1980, where it studied the complex atmosphere and ring system, and made detailed observations of Titan, Saturn's largest moon, whose thick nitrogen atmosphere intrigued scientists. On August 25, 2012, Voyager 1 crossed the heliopause — the boundary where the solar wind gives way to the interstellar medium — becoming the first spacecraft to enter interstellar space. Its twin, Voyager 2, launched 16 days earlier on August 20, 1977, is the only spacecraft to have visited all four giant planets: Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune. Both spacecraft are powered by radioisotope thermoelectric generators (RTGs), which convert heat from the decay of plutonium-238 into electricity, and are expected to continue transmitting data until roughly 2025.\n</source_text>\n\n<previous_summary>\nVoyager 1, launched by NASA aboard a Titan IIIE-Centaur on September 5, 1977, is the most distant human-made object, traveling through interstellar space. It carries a Golden Record showcasing Earth's life and culture, curated by Carl Sagan of Cornell University. In 2012, it became the first spacecraft to cross the heliopause into interstellar space. Voyager 1 studied Saturn’s moon Titan and is powered by radioisotope thermoelectric generators, with transmissions expected until 2025.\n</previous_summary>\n\n<already_extracted_entities>\nVoyager 1, NASA, September 5, 1977, Interstellar space, Golden Record, Carl Sagan, Titan IIIE-Centaur, Titan, Heliopause, Radioisotope thermoelectric generators, Cornell University\n</already_extracted_entities>"} {:role "user", :content "Answer in JSON using this schema:\nCodEntity {\n  // Atomic entity name — a single person, place, date, concept, or thing (required)\n  entity: string,\n  // Brief rationale: why this entity is salient to the text (required)\n  rationale: string,\n  // Salience score from 0.0 to 1.0 — how central this entity is to the text's main point (required)\n  score: float,\n}\n\n{\n  // Salient entities identified in this iteration, each with rationale and salience score (required)\n  entities: CodEntity[],\n  // The summary for this iteration (required)\n  summary: string,\n}"}]}}

clojure.lang.ExceptionInfo: request timed out {:com.blockether.anomaly.core/category :com.blockether.anomaly.core/fault, :com.blockether.anomaly.core/message "request timed out", :type :svar.core/http-error, :llm-request {:model "gpt-4o", :base-url "https://llm.blockether.com/v1/chat/completions", :timeout-ms 180000, :api-key-length 35, :api-key-prefix "sk-bc99e", :messages [{:role "system", :content "<objective>\n<chain_of_density_iteration>\n    <task>Create a denser version of the previous summary by incorporating missing entities.</task>\n    \n    <process>\n        <step number=\"1\">Review the already-extracted entities list — do NOT re-extract any of them</step>\n        <step number=\"2\">Identify salient entities from the SOURCE TEXT that are missing from BOTH the entity list AND the previous summary</step>\n        <step number=\"3\">Rewrite the summary to incorporate these genuinely new entities while maintaining the same word count</step>\n    </process>\n    \n    <entity_criteria>\n    <criterion name=\"atomic\">Each entity must be a single named thing — a person, place, date, organization, concept, or term. NOT a phrase, clause, or description.</criterion>\n    <criterion name=\"relevant\">Related to the main story or central theme</criterion>\n    <criterion name=\"specific\">A proper noun, date, or precise term from the text — not a generic description or paraphrase</criterion>\n    <criterion name=\"faithful\">Must appear verbatim or near-verbatim in the source text — no invented modifiers or rewordings</criterion>\n    <criterion name=\"novel\">Not present in the previous summary or the already-extracted entities list</criterion>\n</entity_criteria>\n    \n    <guidelines>\n        <instruction>Make every word count by rewriting for better flow</instruction>\n        <instruction>Create space through fusion, compression, and removing fillers</instruction>\n        <instruction>Use ONLY information present in the source text — remove any interpretation, framing, or meta-commentary</instruction>\n        <instruction>Do NOT describe what the text is — summarize WHAT IT SAYS</instruction>\n        <instruction>The summary must be self-contained and understandable without the source</instruction>\n        <instruction>Missing entities can appear anywhere in the new summary</instruction>\n        <instruction>Never drop entities from the previous summary</instruction>\n        <instruction>If no space can be made, add fewer new entities rather than dropping old ones</instruction>\n        <constraint>Maintain approximately ~80 words</constraint>\n    </guidelines>\n    \n    <output_requirements>\n        <field name=\"entities\">Array of NEW entity objects added in this iteration, each with 'entity' (atomic name), 'rationale' (why it's salient), and 'score' (0.0-1.0 salience)</field>\n        <field name=\"summary\">The rewritten denser summary (~80 words)</field>\n    </output_requirements>\n</chain_of_density_iteration>\n</objective>"} {:role "user", :content "<source_text>\nVoyager 1, launched by NASA on September 5, 1977, from Cape Canaveral aboard a Titan IIIE-Centaur rocket, is the most distant human-made object from Earth. As of 2024, it is over 24 billion kilometers from Earth, traveling through interstellar space at approximately 17 kilometers per second. The spacecraft carries a Golden Record, a 12-inch gold-plated copper phonograph record containing sounds and images selected to portray the diversity of life and culture on Earth — curated by a committee chaired by Carl Sagan of Cornell University. Voyager 1 made its closest approach to Jupiter on March 5, 1979, discovering volcanic activity on Io and revealing the intricate structure of Jupiter's ring system. It then flew past Saturn on November 12, 1980, where it studied the complex atmosphere and ring system, and made detailed observations of Titan, Saturn's largest moon, whose thick nitrogen atmosphere intrigued scientists. On August 25, 2012, Voyager 1 crossed the heliopause — the boundary where the solar wind gives way to the interstellar medium — becoming the first spacecraft to enter interstellar space. Its twin, Voyager 2, launched 16 days earlier on August 20, 1977, is the only spacecraft to have visited all four giant planets: Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune. Both spacecraft are powered by radioisotope thermoelectric generators (RTGs), which convert heat from the decay of plutonium-238 into electricity, and are expected to continue transmitting data until roughly 2025.\n</source_text>\n\n<previous_summary>\nVoyager 1, launched by NASA aboard a Titan IIIE-Centaur on September 5, 1977, is the most distant human-made object, traveling through interstellar space. It carries a Golden Record showcasing Earth's life and culture, curated by Carl Sagan of Cornell University. In 2012, it became the first spacecraft to cross the heliopause into interstellar space. Voyager 1 studied Saturn’s moon Titan and is powered by radioisotope thermoelectric generators, with transmissions expected until 2025.\n</previous_summary>\n\n<already_extracted_entities>\nVoyager 1, NASA, September 5, 1977, Interstellar space, Golden Record, Carl Sagan, Titan IIIE-Centaur, Titan, Heliopause, Radioisotope thermoelectric generators, Cornell University\n</already_extracted_entities>"} {:role "user", :content "Answer in JSON using this schema:\nCodEntity {\n  // Atomic entity name — a single person, place, date, concept, or thing (required)\n  entity: string,\n  // Brief rationale: why this entity is salient to the text (required)\n  rationale: string,\n  // Salience score from 0.0 to 1.0 — how central this entity is to the text's main point (required)\n  score: float,\n}\n\n{\n  // Salient entities identified in this iteration, each with rationale and salience score (required)\n  entities: CodEntity[],\n  // The summary for this iteration (required)\n  summary: string,\n}"}]}}
